behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is 'analyzer processes all files instead of just changed ones'—a logic error in file selection. A Unit test can mock the 'get changed files' boundary and verify the analyzer is only invoked with the correct subset. Mocking the file source doesn't hide the failure because the actual problem is the filtering logic itself.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the 'get changed files' interface to return {file_a.py, file_b.py}. Mock the analyzer to track which files it processes. Call the lint step with 10 total files in the repository. Assert that the analyzer was invoked exactly 2 times with only the changed files, and never with the other 8 files.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment.
behaviors.justification: This is pure computational logic: given a list of findings, group by severity. A Unit test can verify the grouping logic deterministically. The GitHub API call to post the comment can be mocked. The core failure—findings from different severities mixed in output—is a pure logic error detectable without I/O.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of 10 findings with mixed severities (3 errors, 4 warnings, 3 infos) in random order. Call the grouping function. Assert that findings are grouped into exactly 3 sections (errors, warnings, infos) with no cross-group contamination. Optionally verify ordering stability within groups. Mock the GitHub API call and assert the grouped structure is passed to it.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout.
behaviors.justification: This is a performance test. A Unit test with mocked file I/O and mocked analysis rules would run instantly, hiding the timeout failure entirely. The failure boundary is only crossed when real computation happens. To catch 'analysis takes >10 minutes', we must perform real file I/O and real (or realistic) static analysis. Mocking both dependencies defeats the purpose.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary directory with 200 test files (mix of realistic file types: Python, JavaScript, etc.) containing intentional violations. Run the lint step with all real analysis rules enabled (or a representative subset). Measure elapsed time. Assert completion in <5 minutes locally (allowing buffer for CI resource variation). Verify all 200 files were processed. Collect and count findings. Clean up test directory.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted.
behaviors.justification: The failure is 'when linter crashes, the entire step fails without posting results'—an exception-handling failure. A Unit test can mock the linter to raise an exception on a specific file and verify the code catches it and continues. The core logic (exception handling, iteration, and error recovery) doesn't depend on subprocess infrastructure; mocking the linter boundary is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the linter function to raise an exception when processing 'malformed_file.py'. Configure the lint step to process 5 files including the malformed one. Call the lint step. Assert that: (1) the exception is caught and not re-raised, (2) the other 4 files are still analyzed, (3) findings from all 4 non-malformed files are collected, and (4) results are posted/returned without crashing the step.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output.
behaviors.justification: This is pure deduplication logic. A Unit test can create findings with duplicates and verify the deduplication algorithm deterministically. No I/O, no external calls, no mocking of dependencies—just logic verification. The failure 'duplicates appear in output' is purely computational.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of findings where 2 different rules report the same issue on the same line (e.g., two style checkers both flag line 42 for whitespace). Call the deduplication function. Assert that the output contains one finding for line 42 (or a properly deduplicated representation). Test edge cases: findings on different lines are preserved, findings with different severities on the same line are handled correctly, duplicate findings from the same rule are not removed.
